content.explanation: A static route is a routing table entry an engineer types in by hand — it never changes on its own, even if the path it points to goes down. A dynamic routing protocol (like OSPF or BGP) has routers exchange routing information with each other automatically, so the network re-converges on a new path if a link fails, with no human involved.
content.example:
Static: ip route 172.20.0.0 255.255.0.0 10.1.1.1
  (always points at 10.1.1.1, even if that link is dead)

OSPF (dynamic): routers advertise their connected networks to neighbors;
  if a link fails, neighbors detect it and recalculate a new best path within seconds.

check.statement: Fill in the blank: unlike a static route, a ____ routing protocol like OSPF automatically finds a new path if a link fails.

solution.explanation: Dynamic routing protocols continuously exchange reachability information between routers, so when a link fails the affected routers detect it and recalculate — a static route has no such mechanism and would keep pointing at a dead path until someone fixes it manually.
